does: Patcher introduces a post-hoc defense mechanism that repairs backdoored language models using only a single failure instance without requiring knowledge of the attack method. The framework operates in two stages: first, it localizes backdoor triggers by computing response-conditioned gradient-based saliency scores and applying adaptive clustering to distinguish triggers from benign context; second, it patches the model through constrained fine-tuning that breaks trigger-response associations while preserving performance on legitimate tasks and robustness against non-triggered jailbreaks via KL-divergence constraints. The method requires only access to model parameters and a single reported failure case, making it practical for real-world deployment where comprehensive attack information is unavailable.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature on backdoor attacks against language models and post-hoc defense mechanisms. It extends prior research on gradient-based model inspection and saliency analysis, which have been applied to understand model behavior and identify malicious patterns. The paper responds to limitations of existing defenses that typically require multiple triggered examples or full attack specification, drawing inspiration from adversarial robustness literature on constrained fine-tuning and KL-divergence regularization to maintain model utility. It connects to work on prompt injection attacks and safety alignment failures, positioning backdoor defense as a critical component of securing aligned language models against intentional poisoning.
enables: Patcher opens pathways for practical deployment of post-hoc defenses in production language model systems where defenders encounter isolated failure cases without complete attack metadata. It enables development of more efficient repair mechanisms that balance security against backdoors with preservation of model capabilities, potentially inspiring future work on detecting whether failures stem from backdoors versus natural alignment gaps. The gradient-based trigger localization approach could be extended to detect other forms of hidden model behaviors beyond backdoors, such as deceptive or out-of-distribution capabilities. This framework supports building more resilient safety mechanisms for deployed models and encourages exploration of minimal-information defense strategies applicable across diverse NLP systems.
